flashcards.front: What is an array in C programming?
flashcards.back: An array is a collection of elements of the SAME DATA TYPE stored in contiguous memory locations. It allows storing multiple values under a single variable name.
A one-dimensional array is a linear collection of elements of the same type, accessed using a single index. Syntax: data_type array_name[size];

flashcards.front: What is the index of the first element in an array?
flashcards.back: The first element in a C array is at index 0 (zero-based indexing). The last element is at index (n-1) where n is the array size.

flashcards.front: How is a two-dimensional array stored in memory?
flashcards.back: A two-dimensional array is stored in row-major order in C, meaning elements of each row are stored consecutively, followed by the next row.
Array initialization is assigning initial values to array elements at the time of declaration. Example: int arr[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};

flashcards.front: What happens if fewer initializers are provided than array size?
flashcards.back: If fewer values are provided, remaining elements are automatically initialized to zero (for numeric types) or null characters (for char arrays).

flashcards.front: Can the size of a C array be variable at runtime?
flashcards.back: In standard C, array size must be a constant expression at compile time. Variable Length Arrays (VLA) are supported in C99 but are not recommended for portability.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between array name and pointer?
flashcards.back: In C, the array name (without brackets) acts as a pointer to the first element of the array. arr is equivalent to &arr[0].
A multidimensional array has more than one dimension. A 2D array is like a matrix (rows and columns), while a 3D array can be visualized as a cube of elements.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of using arrays?
flashcards.back: Advantages: 1) Store multiple related values in one variable 2) Easy access to elements using index 3) Efficient iteration using loops 4) Contiguous memory allows fast access.

flashcards.front: What are the limitations of arrays?
flashcards.back: Limitations: 1) Fixed size (must know size at compile time in standard C) 2) Wastage of memory if all elements not used 3) No built-in bounds checking 4) Difficult to insert/delete elements.

flashcards.front: How do you pass an array to a function in C?
flashcards.back: Array name is passed to function (passed by reference). The function receives a pointer to the first element. Size should be passed separately if needed.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between arr[i] and *(arr+i)?
flashcards.back: Both expressions are equivalent. arr[i] uses subscript notation while *(arr+i) uses pointer arithmetic to access the same element.

flashcards.front: How do you declare and initialize a 2D array?
What is the difference between static and dynamic arrays?
flashcards.back: Static arrays have fixed size at compile time and use stack memory. Dynamic arrays are created using pointers and memory allocation functions (malloc, calloc), stored in heap memory.
